RESEARCH ARTICLE
Machine Learning‑Driven Archaeological Site Prediction in the Central 
Part of Jharkhand, India Using Multi‑parametric Geospatial Data
Sanjit Kumar Pal1  · Shubhankar Maity1 · Amit Bera1 · Debajit Ghosh1,2 · Anil Kumar2
Received: 6 May 2024 / Accepted: 11 August 2024 / Published online: 29 August 2024 
© Indian Society of Remote Sensing 2024
Abstract
The central part of Jharkhand, India, harbours a complex history shaped by ancient civilisations, notably Buddhism, Jainism, 
and Brahmanism, necessitating a meticulous identification of potential archaeological sites. This study employs a cutting-
edge machine learning approach to predict the suitability of archaeological sites in the region, marking a significant evolution 
in the documentation of such sites. Machine learning-based integration of 12 geoenvironmental datasets using a random 
forest model reveals a nuanced spatial distribution of potential archaeological sites, categorised into four suitability zones: 
high, moderately high, moderately low, and low. The region with the best-anticipated suitability comprises around 20.33% 
of the research area, whereas the area with the lowest expected suitability comprises nearly 41.81%. High suitability zones, 
characterised by gentle terrain, open vegetation, fertile soils, and water proximity, suggest conditions conducive to human 
habitation and archaeological preservation. Conversely, low suitability zones exhibit rugged terrain, dense vegetation, poor 
soil quality, limited water availability, and remoteness from natural resources, indicating potential hindrances to human 
occupation and archaeological preservation. The model exhibited high predictive accuracy, as evidenced by the ROC–AUC 
score of 88.3%, enhancing its reliability. Specific locations within the study area demonstrate varying degrees of suitabil-
ity, providing valuable insights for archaeological site management, cultural heritage preservation, and land-use planning, 
which will support the restoration and conservation plan of the heritage sites. Furthermore, this machine learning-based 
archaeological site prediction study underscores its potential applicability in historically rich regions globally, showcasing 
its significance in uncovering and preserving our shared human history.
Keywords Geoenvironmental datasets · GIS and remote sensing approach · Prioritising exploration · Random forest 
algorithm · Archaeological site prediction
Introduction
Archaeological site mapping is a critical aspect of under -
standing and preserving cultural heritage. Predicting the 
location of archaeological sites requires a multidisciplinary 
approach that takes into account various factors related 
to geography, geology, and historical context (Siart et al., 
2008). Jharkhand, a region steeped in history and cultural 
heritage, hosts an array of archaeological sites that bear tes-
timony to the rich and diverse past of the land (Sahay, 2005). 
The identification, preservation, and management of these 
sites require comprehensive approaches that amalgamate 
traditional archaeological methods with cutting-edge tech -
nological tools.
In recent years, the application of GIS technology has 
facilitated the collation of spatial data, providing a frame-
work to analyse and interpret geographic information rel-
evant to archaeological sites (Álvarez Larrain & McCall, 
2019; McCoy & Ladefoged, 2009). The integration of 
remote sensing techniques such as satellite imagery and 
aerial photography has enabled the extraction of valuable 
data regarding land-use patterns, topography, and environ-
mental changes, thereby aiding in the detection and char -
acterisation of potential archaeological sites. Numerous 
antecedent investigations have delved into the exploration

of archaeological site prognostication across diverse global 
regions, employing both conventional and sophisticated 
methodologies (Sharafi et al., 2016; Díaz-Rodríguez et al., 
2023; Kanters et al., 2021; Montes et al., 2020; Ruschioni 
et al., 2023). However, a conspicuous lacuna exists, wherein 
only a scant cadre of scholars has ventured into the realm of 
harnessing machine learning algorithms in conjunction with 
a myriad of geoenvironmental parameters for the same pur-
pose. Machine learning (ML) is a rapidly developing field 
with the potential to revolutionise archaeological research 
(Oonk & Spijker, 2015). ML-driven archaeological site pre-
diction is a particularly promising area of research, as it can 
help archaeologists identify new sites and better understand 
the distribution of archaeological sites in a given region 
(Walker et al., 2023; Yaworsky et al., 2020).
Flora and fauna species may be observed distributed dis-
cretely, systematically, or in clusters (Goings, 2010; Nsanzi-
yera et al., 2018a). The choice of certain locations for human 
habitation throughout ancient times was not arbitrary, but 
rather influenced by distinct features of the natural envi -
ronment and variables linked with human behaviour and 
societal conventions (Fenger-Nielsen et al., 2019). River-
based civilisations have been a driving force in human his-
tory (Macklin & Lewin, 2015 ). They have provided reli-
able sources of water, food, and transportation, as well as 
opportunities for trade and cultural exchange. As a result, 
river valleys have been home to some of the most important 
and influential civilisations in the world (Trigger, 2003). 
Archaeological research has shed light on many aspects of 
these civilisations, but there is still much to learn.
Chatra, Hazaribagh, and Ramgarh districts in the Indian 
state of Jharkhand are home to a rich archaeological herit-
age, with a wide range of sites from the Stone Age to the 
medieval period. Over the years, a few interdisciplinary 
scholars have conducted periodic inspections of Jharkhand’s 
megalithic monuments (Ghosh, 2012; Mitra & Bose, 1936; 
Shekhar, 2022; Shekhar et al., 2014; Topno, 1955). The 
Ranchi Plateau in Jharkhand is known for its megalithic sites 
encompassing tall menhirs and dolmens symbolising bur -
ial practices over a large area of land. In this region, many 
tribes, such as Gonds, Mundas, and Oraons, continue to 
engage in the living tradition of megalithic art (Das, 2018). 
Cupules, which are enigmatic relics, have been found on 
rock surfaces and megalithic sites in Jharkhand (Das, 2016). 
The Ranchi Plateau in Jharkhand is home to tall and large 
menhirs known as Birdiris, which were used as memori-
als, for special occasions, or as boundary stones (Ghosh, 
2012). The scattered distribution of stone sculptures across 
Jharkhand’s districts suggests that a significant proportion 
are likely in situ, reflecting their creation within or near their 
current locations (Imam, 2011; India et al., 2005; Neumayer, 
2013; Rajak et al., 2020). The geological diversity of the 
region, encompassing dolerite, granite, mica-rich belts, and 
various metamorphic formations, supports the hypothesis 
that many sculptures were fashioned from locally available 
materials. However, it is plausible that some sculptures 
could have been transported from distant sources, particu-
larly those made from materials not locally abundant.
The significance of this research lies in its potential to 
not only identify potential archaeological sites but also to 
aid cultural heritage preservation and management strate-
gies. The findings and methodologies discussed herein can 
significantly contribute to the broader field of archaeology, 
providing a template for similar studies in different regions 
with rich cultural histories.
Study Area
The study area is situated in the state of Jharkhand, a 
region teeming with historical significance and cultural 
heritage in eastern India. Chatra, Hazaribagh and Ram-
garh, the three focal districts of this research (Fig.  1), are 
emblematic of the diverse historical legacy that Jharkhand 
encapsulates. Located in the central part of the state, 
these districts are characterized by their rich archaeologi-
cal potential, presenting an amalgamation of historical, 
cultural, and environmental intricacies. The relationship 
between rivers and archaeological sites is crucial, as water 
bodies significantly influenced the cultural practices of 
ancient communities. Major rivers and their tributaries 
in the study area include the Lilajan, Barakar, Leriya, 
Mohani, Bokaro, Damodar, Barki, Garhi, Kunur, Barsoti, 
Dhol, and Haharo (Fig. S1, see supplementary file). The 
relationship between roads and archaeological sites in the 
study area is also crucial for comprehending the region's 
historical connectivity and cultural exchange. The major 
road network map is shown in supplementary Fig. S2 
(see supplementary file). Chatra district, nestled amid the 
Chota Nagpur Plateau, is known for its varied topography, 
which is comprised of plateaus, forests, and river valleys. 
Its landscape hosts a mix of historical landmarks, ancient 
artefacts, and remnants that reflect the area’s historical 
evolution, making it an intriguing location for archaeo-
logical research. Hazaribagh and Ramgarh districts, have 
a deep-rooted historical legacy, bearing traces of the pro-
cesses of evolution of past cultures, both indigenous and 
mixed ones, represented through significant historical 
events. Several scholarly efforts have been made to recon-
struct these processes, most of which emphasized high-
lighting the importance of architectural, sculptural and 
epigraphic evidence (French, 1924; Leoshko, 1987; Shek -
har, 2022; Williams, 1974). Numerous archaeological sites 
have been brought to light in the study region’s expanse. 
Specifically, Itkhori, Bihari, Singhnauli, and Karma-Khurd 
are situated within the Itkhori Block in the Chatra district. 
261Journal of the Indian Society of Remote Sensing (January 2025) 53(1):259–276 
In the neighbouring Chouparan Block of the Hazaribagh 
district, noteworthy sites include Hathindar (Fig.  2), 
Mangarh, Sohra-Bigha, and Daihar. Notably, Itkhori is 
renowned for housing the esteemed Bhadrakali Temple. 
Bihari, Singhnauli, and Karma-Khurd sites, located within 
a 10–12 km radius of Itkhori, boast numerous archaeo -
logical mounds and small temples. Daihar and Sohra-
Bigha, situated in the same vicinity, feature two significant 
temples known as Kamala Mata Temple and Maa Samo-
har Temple, respectively. The archaeological landscape of 
Mangarh has been significantly impacted by anthropogenic 
activities, destroying most of the archaeological mounds. 
The topographical diversity, combined with the presence 
of rivers, forests, and varied terrains, contributes to the 
complexity and richness of the archaeological landscape 
in these areas.

Methodology
This paper initiates an exhaustive examination of the meth-
odology utilised to harness GIS and remote sensing in tan-
dem with machine learning for predictive mapping in the 
districts of Chatra, Hazaribagh, and Ramgarh. Its objec-
tive is to explicate the intricate process of data collection, 
analysis, and model development. The study predominantly 
concentrates on the amalgamation of varied datasets, encom-
passing topographical information, satellite imagery, and 
environmental data, to train machine learning models for 
precise predictive mapping (Table  1). Figure  3 illustrates 
the methodological flowchart for the study.
Data Sources and Preparation of Thematic 
Layers
The rigorous field survey undertaken for this study iden-
tified a total of 36 pre-existing archaeological sites in the 
study area (Table S1, see supplementary file). Additionally, 
37 non-existent site points were taken into consideration.

The non-existent site points were selected based on a sys-
tematic spatial sampling strategy to ensure comprehensive 
coverage of the study area. These points were deliberately 
chosen in locations where no archaeological sites have been 
previously recorded, such as the midpoints of river channels 
and high points of hilltops where the likelihood of finding 
archaeological sites is minimal. Consequently, a total of 73 
site points were selected for training and testing data, main-
taining a 70:30 ratio through unbiased sampling strategies.
The data normalisation process involved standardising 
each feature to a uniform scale, specifically by scaling the 
values between 0 and 1. This step prevents features with 
larger numerical ranges from disproportionately influenc-
ing the model, thereby improving its performance. The 
sampling method for selecting the training and testing data 
points was systematic, ensuring that the samples were rep-
resentative of the entire study area and minimising bias.

The relatively small dataset size is acknowledged, and 
several strategies were employed to mitigate the impact on 
the model's ability to generalise. The data was carefully 
curated to ensure high-quality and relevant features, and the 
random forest (RF) algorithm was chosen for its robustness 
and ability to handle small datasets effectively. Extensive 
parameter tuning and validation were conducted to optimise 
model performance. Although a larger dataset is generally 
preferable, the available data was leveraged efficiently to 
produce meaningful results.
For the location map, the shapefiles delineating the 
administrative boundaries of both India and Jharkhand 
were sourced from the Survey of India website (https:// onlin 
emaps. surve yofin dia. gov. in/). In this study, 12 thematic data 
sets were employed, including elevation, slope, aspect, cur-
vature, land use/land cover, geology, geomorphology, dis-
tance from the road, distance from the river, distance from 
the pond, soil texture, and the Topographic Wetness Index 
(TWI). Geomorphology and geology maps were sourced 
from the Bhukosh geoportal of the Geological Survey of 
India (GSI) at a scale of 1:50,000. The Digital Elevation 
Model (DEM) was generated using data from the Shut-
tle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM), providing global 
digital data at a spatial resolution of 30 m. Elevation, slope, 
aspect, curvature, and TWI maps were created using the 
DEM within the ArcGIS environment.

To prepare distance maps of roads, rivers, and ponds, 
these features were digitised from Survey of India topo-
graphical sheets spanning from 1977–88 to 2004–2010. The 
selected toposheets for road analysis were chosen for their 
detailed and reliable depiction of geographical features dur-
ing these periods. Analysing the road network from recent 
years helps predict ancient archaeological sites by aiding 
accessibility studies and understanding potential ancient 
routes (Verhagen et al., 2019). Modern infrastructure fre-
quently aligns with historical pathways due to geographical 
constraints, suggesting that ancient sites may be located in 
proximity to current roads. However, it’s crucial to acknowl-
edge limitations. Modern roads might deviate significantly 
from ancient routes due to advancements in technology and 
changing priorities in infrastructure development.
The final maps were generated using the Euclidean dis -
tance tools of ArcGIS 10.5. Soil texture data was obtained

from the National Bureau of Soil Survey and Land Use 
Planning (NBSS and LUP). The land use/land cover map 
was prepared from the Esri-Sentinel-2 10 m Land Use/Land 
Cover map. All the maps were projected in the WGS 1984 
UTM Zone 45N coordinate system, maintaining a consistent 
cell size of 30 m. The threshold values of each thematic layer 
have been categorised using the Jenks natural breaks algo-
rithm (Jenks, 1967), specifically in the case of quantitative 
data layers. This method is recognised for its effectiveness in 
data classification, minimising variance within classes and 
maximising variance between classes. Previous literature on 
archaeological site prediction has also been referenced to 
inform the thresholds. Furthermore, the existing site loca-
tions have been correlated with each subclass to validate the 
categorisation. This dual approach, utilising a well-estab-
lished algorithm and empirical validation, provides a logical 
and evidence-based foundation for the threshold values used 
in the study.
The methodology for assessing the importance of pre-
dictors in the Random Forest model involved analysing the 
Pearson correlation coefficient to evaluate the relationship 
between conditioning factors and the Archaeological Site 
Suitability Prediction map. This approach helped identify 
the most influential variables among the 12 considered, 
thereby validating the model's effectiveness in predicting 
archaeological site locations. Specifically, the correlation 
analysis clarified which variables significantly influenced 
the model predictions.
Random Forest
The random forest (RF) algorithm has proven to be a power-
ful tool commonly employed in classification applications, 
utilised by numerous researchers for predicting archaeologi-
cal sites (Breiman, 2001 ; Castiello & Tonini, 2021; Elliot 
et al., 2021; Li et al., 2023; Walker et al., 2023; Yaworsky 
et al., 2020). This algorithm utilises the bagging method 
that randomly subsetted the training data to create several 
independent decision trees and assign favourable classes 
(Denisko & Hoffman, 2018). The best class is selected based 
on the highest ranking across all decision trees, resulting in 
the creation of the final RF classification map (Rodriguez-
Galiano et al., 2015). Samples excluded from model training 
in the bagging procedure are added to an extra subset known 
as out-of-bag (OB). The validation method used to select 
samples and conduct model training was the out-of-bag error 
estimation inherent to the random forest algorithm (Peters 
et al., 2007). This technique involves using a subset of the 
data that was not included in the bootstrap sample to vali-
date each decision tree, providing an unbiased estimate of 
the model's performance (Rodriguez-Galiano et al., 2015). 
265Journal of the Indian Society of Remote Sensing (January 2025) 53(1):259–276 
Additionally, cross-validation was performed to further 
assess the model's robustness and accuracy.
We executed the RF model code in R (version 4.3.1) to 
predict the archaeological sites in this study. Two dominant 
parameters, the number of trees (nTree) and the number of 
randomly sampled variables (mTry) need to be optimised to 
improve the accuracy of the RF model (Breiman, 2001). This 
study attempted a range of values spanning from 0 to 1000 
for nTree and from 1 to 10 for mTry. The optimal values for 
these two parameters were determined as nTree = 100 and 
mTry = 5 based on the highest accuracy of the RF model. 
Certainly, during the initial stages of computation, a portion 
of the original dataset is typically set aside and excluded 
from the construction of the learning model. This subset is 
later employed in the final step to evaluate the algorithm’s 
proficiency, trained on the training dataset, in making accu-
rate predictions on an untouched testing dataset.
Result and Discussion
Distance from the River
Rivers played a pivotal role in the establishment of early 
human settlements, providing essential resources such as 
water for drinking, cooking, and irrigation, fertile soil for 
agriculture; and transportation routes for trade and cultural 
exchange (Breeze et al., 2015; Gibling, 2018). Archaeo-
logical studies have consistently demonstrated a higher 
density of archaeological sites near rivers (Parow-Souchon 
et al., 2022; Walker et al., 2023). In the context of develop-
ing predictive maps for archaeological sites, the five class 
ranges (0–1 km, 1–2 km, 2–3 km, 3–4 km, 4–5 km) provide 
a framework for assessing the likelihood of encountering 
archaeological sites based on their distance from a river 
(Fig. 4a). Sites within the 0–2 km range exhibit the highest 
probability of archaeological discovery due to their imme-
diate access to water resources and the fertile soils along 
riverbanks. A total of 26 existing sites (72.22%) are located 
within these buffer zones. Sites within the 3–5 km range 
indicate a lower potential for archaeological discovery, with 
only 2.78% of existing sites falling within these zones.
Distance from the Road
Historically, transportation networks, including roads, 
have significantly shaped human settlement patterns and 
cultural interactions. Roads facilitated the movement of 
people, goods, and ideas, influencing the establishment 
of settlements, trade routes, and communication chan -
nels. Archaeological sites are often found in close prox-
imity to ancient roads, reflecting the importance of these 
transportation networks in past societies (Avner, 1984 ; 
Siart et al., 2008). The classification into different dis -
tance bands, ranging from immediate proximity to roads 
(0–1 km) to more remote areas (10–22 km), allows for a 
nuanced analysis (Fig.  4b). Sites closer to roads (0–3 km) 
might reveal evidence of smaller settlements or points of 
interest, while those situated further away (6–22 km) may 
indicate more remote or less-travelled areas where differ -
ent types of archaeological discoveries, like ancient trade 
routes or undiscovered settlements, could be found. A total 
of 21 existing sites (58.3%) are situated within the 0–1 km 
zones. In the 1–3 km range, 13.9% of sites are found, 
while 22.2% of sites fall within the 3–6 km range. Sites 
located within the 6–22 km range show a lower potential 
for archaeological discovery, with only 5.6% of existing 
sites situated in these zones.
Elevation
Elevation serves as a fundamental factor influencing the 
distribution and occurrence of historical settlements or 
artefacts (Siart et al., 2008). Elevation can influence a 
variety of environmental factors that were important to 
past human populations, such as climate, vegetation, and 
access to water and other resources. The elevation cut-
off values were determined using the Jenks natural breaks 
algorithm (Jenks, 1967), segmenting the study region 
into six distinct class ranges: 147–282 m, 282–370 m, 
370–431 m, 431–502 m, 502–600 m, and 600–1053 m. 
This method provides a detailed analysis of how different 
elevation bands correlate with the presence of archaeo-
logical sites. 21 existing sites (58.3%) are found within 
the 370–431 m elevation range. However, no archaeologi-
cal sites have been recorded within the elevation range 
of 1470–370 m. Moderately low elevations (370–431 m) 
typically have more fertile soils and proximity to water 
sources, making them ideal for early human settlement 
and agriculture. These conditions increase the likelihood 
of discovering pottery fragments and megaliths, which are 
often associated with habitational and burial sites (Wang 
et al., 2023). 9 sites (25%) fall within the 431–502 m ele-
vation range. In contrast, only 8.3% of sites are found at 
elevations greater than 600 m, which is less favourable for 
predicting archaeological sites. Modern settlements and 
extensive agricultural activities in the central highlands, at 
elevations greater than 370 m and up to 1053 m, contrast 
with historical patterns. Technological advancements in 
modern agriculture, including improved irrigation and soil 
management, have enabled successful farming at higher 
elevations (Chhetri et al., 2012). Modern settlement pat-
terns are influenced by infrastructure, market access, and 
socio-economic factors, differing from the resource-based 
decisions of early human populations.

Slope
The inclination of the slope plays a pivotal role in com-
prehending historical human settlement and endeavours 
within a designated region. This study area is categorised 
into six distinct classes of slope angles: 0–3°, 3–5°, 5–10°, 
10–15°, 15–25°, and 25–68° (Fig.  4d). Gentle gradients, 
falling within the 0–5° range, are often held in high regard 
due to their propensity for potential archaeological sites. 
This slope range covers 77.3% of the study area. 83.3% (30 
sites) of the existing archaeological sites fall within this 
slope range. Such subtle inclines offer conducive condi-
tions for human dwelling, construction, and agricultural 
practices, thereby serving as primary sites for unearth -
ing artefacts, megaliths, pottery, and vestiges of ancient 
communities (Nsanziyera et al., 2018b). As the angle of 
inclination increases, the viability for human inhabitation 
decreases. Steeper gradients, between 25 and 68°, pose

Slope Aspect
The aspect map serves a critical role in predicting archaeo-
logical sites by influencing microclimates, sunlight expo-
sure, and temperature distributions across landscapes 
Fig. 4  (continued)
268 Journal of the Indian Society of Remote Sensing (January 2025) 53(1):259–276
(Guechi et al., 2023; Noviello et al., 2018). These factors 
significantly impact the preservation conditions of archaeo-
logical artefacts. For instance, organic materials like wood, 
bone, and textiles are better preserved in arid and sheltered 
settings, whereas metal artefacts are more prone to corro-
sion in damp environments (Chapman et al., 2006; Gerwin 
& Baumhauer, 2000). In this study, the slope aspect was 
delineated into north, northwest, northeast, east, southwest, 
southeast, west, south, and flat orientations (Fig. 4e). South-
ern and southeastern aspects tend to bask in increased sun-
light, offering warmth and fostering conditions conducive 
to agriculture and human habitation (Tong et al., 2022). 
Conversely, northern and northwestern aspects tend to be 
cooler and receive diminished direct sunlight, potentially 
rendering them less appealing for ancient settlements due to 
the severity of environmental conditions. Southwest-facing 
slopes, though benefitting from enhanced sunlight compared 
to south-facing slopes, contend with heightened exposure to 
the elements (Xi et al., 2018).
Land Use/Land Cover
Various land use and cover types (Fig.  4f) offer significant 
insights into historical human activities and potential archae-
ological site locations. Barren land, often present in regions 
with past geological activity or substantial erosion, serves 
as a promising indicator for potential archaeological sites, as 
weathering exposes stone tools and artefacts in such areas. 
Scrub or grassland areas are categorised under the range-
land category. Rangeland and fallow areas, akin to barren 
land, possess the potential for preserving ancient sites due 
to minimal disruption, potentially tied to specific activities 
of ancient societies (Ullah, 2011). Water sources historically 
held immense importance for human survival, making them 
ideal locations to search for evidence of ancient settlements 
(Mays et al., 2007), including fish bones and shell middens 
(Habu et al., 2011). Forested areas serve as promising sites 
for archaeological discoveries (Davis et al., 2019), as they 
might house a range of preserved artefacts in the soil or 
beneath the dense tree cover. However, the dense vegeta-
tion might impede visibility and exploration. The vegeta-
tion covering steep slopes (> 25°) consists predominantly 
of forested areas, including tropical and deciduous forests 
dominated by species such as Shorea robusta (Sal). Continu-
ous disturbance in agricultural lands significantly impacts 
potential site preservation, with ploughing and agricultural 
activities potentially affecting the integrity of megaliths and 
other structures (Sadovnik et al., 2014).
Distance from the Ponds and Lakes
Water sources, such as ponds and lakes, have played a pivotal 
role in attracting and sustaining early human settlements. 
Archaeological studies have consistently shown that archae-
ological sites are more densely concentrated near water 
sources (Jodry & Stanford, 1996; Pardoe, 2003). This trend 
is particularly pronounced in arid and semi-arid regions, 
where water scarcity makes it an essential resource for sur -
vival. Five class ranges (0–1 km, 1–3 km, 3–5 km, 5–10 km, 
and10–15 km) have been considered to assess the likelihood 
of archaeological sites based on their distance from a pond 
(Fig. 4g). The highest probability of encountering sites lies 
within the 0–1 km and 1–3 km ranges, as these locations 
were most desirable for drinking, cooking, and agricultural 
purposes. Within these ranges, 18 existing sites (50%) fall 
under the 0–1 km class, while 13 sites (36.11%) fall under 
the 1–3 km class. Conversely, sites within the 10–15 km 
range have a lower probability of containing archaeological 
remains, as no existing sites are present within this class.
Soil Texture
Soil composition plays a crucial role in anticipating the loca-
tions of archaeological sites (Ben-Romdhane et al., 2023; 
Gillreath-Brown et al., 2019). Typically, archaeological 
sites are situated in regions characterized by well-drained 
soils conducive to agriculture and settlement (Fedick & 
Ford, 1990). In the study region, the six mentioned soil 
types exhibit distinct textures and drainage characteristics 
(Fig. 4h). Loamy and loamy-skeletal soils emerge as particu-
larly advantageous for archaeological sites due to their effec-
tive drainage and high fertility, rendering them well-suited 
for agricultural and other human activities. Fine loamy and 
fine silty soils contribute to a robust soil structure and effec-
tive water retention, creating conditions more conducive to 
the enduring preservation of artefacts, marking them as 
moderately high-potential soil types. Silty loam soil, on the 
other hand, exhibits a marginal potential compared to coarse 
loamy soil.
Curvature
Topographic curvature helps in understanding the terrain 
morphology, including convex and concave features, which 
can influence the preservation and visibility of archaeologi-
cal remains (Cerrillo-Cuenca, 2017; De Reu et al., 2011; 
Verhagen & Drăguţ, 2012). Convex areas, characterised by 
an outward curve, tend to be stable and less prone to ero-
sion compared to concave areas, which exhibit an inward 
curvature (Fig. 4i). This stability suggests that convex areas 
are more likely to preserve archaeological materials in their 
original positions (Albert et al., 2008). These areas are also 
typically more visible on the surface, facilitating their iden-
tification during archaeological surveys. Convex areas may 
indicate higher ground suitable for settlements or defen-
sive structures. Conversely, concave areas are susceptible 
269Journal of the Indian Society of Remote Sensing (January 2025) 53(1):259–276 
to erosion and sedimentation, which can obscure and alter 
archaeological materials over time. Areas with flat curvature 
indicate relatively level or near-level terrain. While flat areas 
may be suitable for agriculture and human habitation, they 
are vulnerable to erosion and sediment deposition, poten-
tially burying or erasing archaeological remains.
Topographic Wetness Index
Archaeological sites frequently display a preference for spe-
cific environmental characteristics, and the topographic wet-
ness index (TWI) assists in pinpointing such areas by quan-
tifying the moisture availability in the landscape (Märker & 
Heydari-Guran, 2009; Mertel et al., 2018). By considering 
the nuanced variations in landscape moisture, researchers 
can hone their focus on areas that closely align with histori-
cal human settlement preferences. This approach not only 
aids in streamlining field surveys by narrowing potential 
locations but also contributes to a more comprehensive 
understanding of past human–environment interactions. 
The study region, is characterised by five distinct TWI class 
ranges (1.33–6.66, 6.66–8.81, 8.81–11.61, 11.61–15.35 and 
15.35–25.16) (Fig. 4j). The highest probability of encounter-
ing sites lies within the 1.33–8.81 TWI ranges. Within these 
ranges, 23 existing sites (63.9%) are found, while 7 sites 
(19.4%) fall under the 8.81–11.61 TWI class. Conversely, 
sites within the 11.61–25.16 TWI range have a lower prob-
ability of containing archaeological remains, with only 6 
existing sites (16.7%) present within this class.
Geomorphology
Predicting the existence of Archaeological sites within 
diverse terrains (Fig.  4k) necessitates the contemplation 
of distinct landscape parameters. Valley fills frequently 
furnish comparably even and fertile areas, which might 
have been sought after by ancient societies for settlement 
due to the availability of arable land, water sources, and 
natural protection conferred by surrounding elevations (Lee 
& French, 2016). The Pediment-Corestone-Tor Composite 
landform often denotes a fusion of gently sloping hillsides 
and rocky outcrops. This composite could be conducive 
to megalithic sites due to the inclusion of rocky outcrops, 
tors, or geological formations that might have held signifi-
cance to ancient cultures (Martínez-Pabello et al., 2021). 
Inselbergs, rising abruptly from a plain, might have been 
deemed sacred or strategically vital, rendering them poten-
tial sites for megalithic structures (Kolawole & Anifowose, 
2011). Active quarry regions might not be conducive to 
forecasting megalithic sites, given that activities in such 
areas can obliterate or obscure archaeological evidence 
(Isaac, 1971). Moderately dissected hills and valleys 
emerge as promising locales (Tamang et al., 2023). Older 
floodplains and the pediment-pediplain complexes might 
safeguard archaeological treasures, as these areas histori-
cally teemed with resources, rendering them appealing for 
ancient human activity (Jing et al.,1997; Polley et al., 2023). 
Terrains like gullied lands might pose challenges owing to 
erosion, potentially diminishing the probability of encoun-
tering well-preserved megaliths (García-Ruiz et al., 2021). 
Ancient floodplains might be advantageous locales owing 
to their abundant resources and the likelihood of ancient 
human activity.
Geology
The diverse array of rock types in the study area, encom-
passing newer and older alluvium, dolerite, granite, Chho-
tanagpur Gneissic Complex, Bihar mica belt, pegmatite and 
quartz vein, laterite, unclassified metamorphics, upper and 
lower Gondwana, substantially influence the anticipation of 
archaeological sites (Fig. 4l). Newer alluvium, often located 
in river valleys and floodplains, likely held significance for 
ancient settlements despite its susceptibility to erosion and 
flooding (Guccione, 2008). In contrast, older alluvium, also 
situated in similar topographical settings, is more consoli-
dated, potentially preserving archaeological sites. Megaliths 
possibly found near rivers or streams might be associated 
with these alluvial deposits. Dolerite and granite, intrusive 
igneous rocks, were favoured for constructing megalithic 
structures due to their durability (Moore, 2004). Granite, 
often linked with quartz veins, contains valuable minerals 
like gold and silver (Botros, 2015). The Bihar mica belt, rich 
in mica, an essential mineral for pottery and other appli-
cations, hosts archaeology (Newman, 1992). Lower Gond-
wana, deposited in the Carboniferous and Permian periods, 
houses sedimentary rocks like shale, sandstone, conglomer-
ates, coal, and limestone, offering valuable coal deposits. 
Similarly, Upper Gondwana, formed during the Triassic and 
Jurassic periods, contains coal, iron ore, and other minerals, 
potentially housing megaliths, especially in lowland areas 
(Shekhar, 2021). Laterite, a residual soil from rock weather-
ing in warm climates found in hilly terrains, was utilised in 
constructing megalithic structures (Wadhawan, 2022).
Archaeological Site Suitability Prediction
The spatial distribution of archaeological site suitability 
can be attributed to various factors, including topography, 
vegetation cover, soil characteristics, water availability, 
and proximity to natural resources. High suitability zones 
often coincide with areas of gentle terrain, open vegetation, 
fertile soils, and access to water sources. These conditions 
are conducive to human habitation and the preservation of 
archaeological remains. However, these conditions may 
not necessarily reflect the environment of ancient buried 
270 Journal of the Indian Society of Remote Sensing (January 2025) 53(1):259–276
archaeological sites. Factors such as sedimentation, erosion, 
and changes in hydrology over time could significantly alter 
the landscape’s suitability for archaeological preservation. 
On the other hand, low suitability zones often correspond to 
areas with rugged terrain, dense vegetation, poor soil qual-
ity, limited water availability, and remoteness from natu-
ral resources. These conditions may have hindered human 
occupation and reduced the preservation of archaeological 
evidence. The results of the archaeological site suitability 
analysis reveal a distinct spatial distribution across the study 
area, categorizing it into four predicted suitability zones: 
high, moderately high, moderately low, and low (Fig.  5). 
These zones offer valuable insights into the potential pres-
ence of archaeological sites and contribute to the understand-
ing of the historical and cultural landscape. The highest pre-
dicted suitability zone encompasses approximately 20.33% 
of the study area, comprising specific locations within the 
Chatra, Hazaribagh, and Ramgarh districts. Notably, in the 
Chatra district, Mayurhand, Simaria, Itkhori, and Tandwa 
blocks exhibit a high degree of suitability. In Hazaribagh 
district, Chauparan, Barhi, Ichak, Bishungarh, Hazaribag, 
and Katamdag emerge as areas with high archaeological site 
suitability. Similarly, Dulmi, Gola, and Patratu are identified 
within the Ramgarh district as high suitability zones. Con-
versely, the low suitability zone covers a significant portion 
of the study area, comprising 41.81% of the total. Specific 
areas within the Chatra, Hazaribagh, and Ramgarh districts 
demonstrate lower predicted suitability. In Chatra district, 
Kunda, Lawalaung, and Pathalgora blocks are identified as 
low suitability areas. Within the Hazaribagh district, Ker -
edari, Barkagaon, Katkamsandi, and Churchu exhibit lower 
suitability for archaeological sites. In Ramgarh district, 
Chitarpur and Mandu blocks are also classified as low suit-
ability zones. The moderately high (18.23%) and moderately 
low (19.63%) suitability zones provide additional nuances 
to the spatial distribution, offering a comprehensive view 
of the archaeological potential across the study area. The 
findings contribute valuable information for archaeological 
site management, cultural heritage preservation, and land-
use planning in the region. Researchers, policymakers, and 
local communities can utilise these results to prioritise areas 
for further exploration, conservation efforts, and sustainable 
development, thereby enhancing our understanding of the 
historical and cultural significance of the landscape.
The analysis of the Archaeological site suitability predic-
tion model reveals significant insights into the spatial dis-
tribution of archaeological sites within the study area. The 
model’s prediction accuracy is reflected in the clustering 
of existing archaeological sites within high and moderately 
high suitability zones. Specifically, 58.3% (21 sites) of the

existing sites fall within the high suitability zone, highlight-
ing the model's effectiveness in identifying areas with a high 
likelihood of archaeological significance. This concentration 
suggests that the factors influencing site suitability, such as 
proximity to water sources, fertile soils, and favourable ter-
rain, are well-captured by the model. The moderately high 
suitability zone contains 11% of the existing sites, further 
reinforcing the model's predictive power. The moderately 
low and low suitability zones, encompassing 16.7% (6 sites) 
and 13.9% (5 sites) of existing sites respectively, indicate 
areas where the likelihood of discovering archaeological 
sites decreases. This gradient of site probability underscores 
the model's ability to distinguish between varying degrees 
of archaeological potential across the landscape. Moreover, 
the analysis of non-existent site points provides a critical 
validation of the model. With 94.59% (35 locations) of these 
points falling within low suitability zones, the model effec-
tively minimizes false positives, ensuring that areas with 
low archaeological potential are accurately identified. This 
high rate of non-existent site points in low suitability zones 
further corroborates the model's reliability.
The Pearson correlation coefficient was analysed to 
assess the relationship between the conditioning factors 
and the Archaeological Site Suitability Prediction (ASSP) 
map (Fig.  6). A strong negative correlation (r = − 0.76) 
was observed for LULC, indicating that certain land cover 
types are highly predictive of archaeological site locations. 
Geology showed a strong positive correlation (r = 0.70). 
Aspect presented a moderately strong negative correla -
tion (r = − 0.62), implying that the orientation of the land 
surface significantly influences site suitability. Soil texture 
also exhibited a moderately strong negative correlation 
(r = − 0.64), highlighting the importance of soil properties 
in archaeological site distribution. A moderately strong posi-
tive correlation (r = 0.50) was found for the distance from 
ponds, indicating that proximity to ponds is a consider -
able factor in determining site locations. Geomorphology 
showed a positive correlation (r = 0.53). Conversely, weaker 
correlations were identified for other parameters. Curvature 
displayed a weak negative correlation (r = − 0.30), while 
slope exhibited a weak positive correlation (r = 0.32). Very 
weak negative correlations were found for the distance from 
rivers (r = − 0.02) and the distance from roads (r = − 0.19). 
The TWI showed a very weak positive correlation (r = 0.04). 
These statistical analyses underpin the importance of vari-
ous environmental factors in the model. Parameters with 
strong and moderately strong correlations, such as LULC, 
geology, aspect, soil texture, and geomorphology, were 
assigned higher importance in the ASSP model. Conversely, 
parameters with weak or very weak correlations were given 
lower importance. This approach ensures that the model 
accurately reflects the most influential factors in predicting

Validation
The predictive accuracy of the model was verified through 
a ROC curve analysis, utilising 22 (30%) of the testing data 
sites. A comparison was drawn between these 22 sites and 
the predicted archaeological site suitability map. This clas-
sification aims to ascertain the likelihood of discovering an 
archaeological site (“yes”) or the absence thereof (“no”). A 
numeric assignment of ‘1’ denotes acceptance of the condi-
tion, while a ‘0’ indicates rejection of the conditions. Previ-
ous researchers have used the ROC curve, a well-established 
scientific approach, to verify the accuracy of the predicted 
archaeological maps generated by the Random forest model 
(Castiello & Tonini, 2021; Li et al., 2022, 2023; Yaworsky 
et al., 2020). It is recognized as both a deterministic and 
probabilistic forecasting system. The Area Under the Curve 
(AUC) ranges from 0.5 to 1, with a value nearing 1 signify-
ing a heightened level of predictive accuracy. The prediction 
accuracy rate is divided into five ranges: 0.5–0.6 (low to 
poor), 0.6–0.7 (moderate/average), 0.7–0.8 (good), 0.8–0.9 
(very good), and 0.9–1 (excellent).
Initially, four different machine learning models were 
evaluated to predict archaeological site suitability. Based 
on their performance metrics, including ROC–AUC scores, 
Random Forest emerged as the best-fitted model with a score 
of 88.3%. In comparison, the Neural Network achieved a 
ROC–AUC of 69.1%, Logistic Regression of 70.1%, and 
Support Vector Machine of 78.5%. These results highlight 
the effectiveness and superior predictive power of the Ran-
dom Forest algorithm in our study. Therefore, the Random 
Forest algorithm was considered for the final study.
The ROC–AUC value of 0.883 highlights the model's 
excellent discrimination capability between the presence and 
absence of archaeological sites (Fig.  7). In addition to the 
AUC curve, a confusion matrix has been included to provide 
a more comprehensive accuracy assessment (Fig.  8). The 
confusion matrix reveals that the model demonstrates high 
precision with a score of 91%, indicating that the majority of 
predicted archaeological sites were indeed actual sites. How-
ever, the recall is 71.4%, meaning that the model correctly 
identified approximately 71.4% of all actual sites. Addition-
ally, the model shows high specificity (87.5%), accurately 
identifying non-site locations. The overall accuracy of the 
model stands at 77.21%, and the F1 score is 80%, balanc-
ing precision and recall. These metrics confirm the model’s 
robustness and reliability, with no clear signs of overfitting. 
Archaeological site suitability predictions have been borne 
out by field investigations, lending more credence to the 
model. After generating the archaeological suitability map, a 
pilot field survey was conducted according to the suitability 
predictions. During this survey, a total of 13 locations were 
identified as archaeological sites (Fig. S3, see supplemen-
tary file). The distribution of these sites according to the 
suitability zones is as follows: Eight locations (61.5%) fall 
within the high suitability zone, including Birbir, Banadag, 
Guruwa, Baliandh, Bariyath, Katiya, Kaitha Temple, and 
Kila Temple. Two locations (15.4%), Rola and Gola, fall 
within the moderately high suitability zone. Another two 
locations (15.4%), Bhagwantola and Benti, fall within the 
moderately low suitability zone. Finally, one location (7.7%), 
Garh Bandh in Ramgarh district, falls within the low suit-
ability zone. These results validate the performance of the 
Random Forest model used in the study, as a significant pro-
portion of the predicted high and moderately high suitability

based on the predicted suitability map.
Conclusions
This paper presents the results of an ML-driven archaeologi-
cal site prediction study in Chatra, Hazaribagh and Ram-
garh. The study used a random forest algorithm to predict 
the probability of archaeological sites based on a dataset 
of known archaeological sites and a variety of geoenviron-
mental factors. The results reveal a distinct spatial distribu-
tion across the study area, categorising it into four predicted 
suitability zones: high, moderately high, moderately low, 
and low. The highest predicted suitability zone encompasses 
approximately 20.33% of the study area, while the low suita-
bility zone covers a substantial 41%, providing a comprehen-
sive overview of archaeological potential. Specific locations 
within Chatra, Hazaribagh, and Ramgarh districts exhibit 
varying degrees of suitability, with notable high suitability 
areas identified in Chatra’s Mayurhand, Simaria, Itkhori, and 
Tandwa blocks, Hazaribagh’s Chauparan, Barhi, Ichak, Bis-
hungarh, Hazaribag, and Katamdag, and Ramgarh’s Dulmi, 
Gola and Patratu. High suitability zones, characterised by 
gentle terrain, open vegetation, fertile soils, and proximity 
to water sources, manifest conditions conducive to human 
habitation and the preservation of archaeological remnants. 
Conversely, low suitability zones, marked by rugged terrain, 
dense vegetation, poor soil quality, limited water availability, 
and remoteness from natural resources, suggest conditions 
that may have impeded human occupation and diminished 
the preservation of archaeological evidence. The study also 
found that the model used to predict archaeological site suit-
ability was highly accurate, as evidenced by the ROC–AUC 
score of 88.3%. This suggests that the model can be used to 
reliably identify areas that are likely to contain archaeologi-
cal sites. The findings of this study have important impli-
cations for archaeological research. The findings perhaps 
provide various decision-making processes, such as archaeo-
logical site management, cultural heritage preservation, and 
land-use planning in the Chatra, Hazaribagh and Ramgarh 
districts. It can also be used to guide field surveys to areas 
where the probability of new archaeological sites is high. 
The study also demonstrates the potential of ML for archaeo-
logical site prediction in other parts of India and the world.
